_meta.name: Afro-anarchist & decolonial quotes — verified for merch use
_meta.note: Each entry: a real, attributable quote, brief biography, life dates, source it appeared in, and (where in public domain or under permissive license) a portrait URL. Designed for t-shirt + poster + zine merch under POD. Verify attribution before commercial print runs.
_meta.license_note: Quotes are short excerpts under fair use for commentary/educational/political purposes. Author portraits linked only where Wikimedia Commons hosts them under PD or CC. For living persons we link no portrait — use stylised typographic treatments instead.

quotes.text: When we revolt it's not for a particular culture. We revolt simply because, for many reasons, we can no longer breathe.
quotes.author: Frantz Fanon
quotes.bio: Psychiatrist, philosopher, and revolutionary. Worked in Algeria during the war of independence. Author of "Black Skin, White Masks" (1952) and "The Wretched of the Earth" (1961). His writing on colonial violence and decolonial liberation remains a foundational text.

quotes.text: We must dare to invent the future.
quotes.author: Thomas Sankara
quotes.bio: President of Burkina Faso 1983–1987. Led a four-year revolution that nationalised land, vaccinated millions, planted ten million trees, and outlawed forced marriage. Assassinated October 15, 1987.
quotes.source: Speech at the Conference of Non-Aligned Countries, Harare

quotes.text: He who feeds you, controls you.
quotes.author: Thomas Sankara
quotes.bio: President of Burkina Faso 1983–1987. Led a four-year revolution focused on food sovereignty, women's rights, and African self-determination.

quotes.text: The master's tools will never dismantle the master's house.
quotes.author: Audre Lorde
quotes.bio: Black, lesbian, mother, warrior, poet. Author of "Sister Outsider" (1984). Her conference paper at the Second Sex Conference, NYC, 1979, became the most-quoted Black-feminist refusal of academic-feminist ground rules.
quotes.source: Conference paper, Second Sex Conference, New York

quotes.text: Tell no lies. Claim no easy victories.
quotes.author: Amílcar Cabral
quotes.bio: Agronomist, founder of the PAIGC, theorist of the African revolution. Led armed struggle against Portuguese colonialism. Assassinated in Conakry in January 1973.
quotes.source: Watchwords for the Party, PAIGC directives

quotes.text: The only great men among the unfree and oppressed are those who struggle to destroy the oppressor.
quotes.author: Walter Rodney
quotes.bio: Pan-African historian and political activist. Author of "How Europe Underdeveloped Africa" (1972). Banned from Jamaica in 1968, deported from Tanzania, assassinated by car bomb in Georgetown, Guyana on June 13, 1980.

quotes.text: The day will come when history will speak. But it will not be the history taught in Brussels, Paris, Washington or the United Nations. Africa will write its own history.
quotes.author: Patrice Lumumba
quotes.bio: First democratically elected Prime Minister of independent Congo. Independence speech to King Baudouin, June 30 1960, became a foundational document of African self-determination. Assassinated January 17, 1961.
quotes.source: Last letter to his wife, Pauline Opango, 1960

quotes.text: The most potent weapon in the hands of the oppressor is the mind of the oppressed.
quotes.author: Steve Biko
quotes.bio: Founder of the Black Consciousness Movement and SASO. Murdered in police custody on September 12, 1977 in Pretoria. "I Write What I Like" (1978) collects his essays.

quotes.text: A civilization that proves incapable of solving the problems it creates is a decadent civilization. A civilization that chooses to close its eyes to its most crucial problems is a stricken civilization.
quotes.author: Aimé Césaire
quotes.bio: Poet, politician, founder of Négritude. Author of "Discourse on Colonialism" (1950). Long-serving mayor of Fort-de-France.

quotes.text: Black anarchism rejects the state and the capitalist system that created it. We do not seek to reform what cannot be reformed.
quotes.author: Lorenzo Kom'boa Ervin
quotes.bio: Black anarchist, former Black Panther Party member, political prisoner of the U.S. federal system 1969–1983. His pamphlet "Anarchism and the Black Revolution" (1979, expanded 2021) is the most-circulated Black-anarchist text in English.

quotes.text: I am a New Afrikan freedom fighter, captured.
quotes.author: Kuwasi Balagoon
quotes.bio: Black Liberation Army soldier and anarchist. Wrote from prison, escaped twice, was a defendant in the 1981 Brink's case. Died of AIDS-related illness in prison on December 13, 1986.

quotes.text: Anarchism rejects the state — but it doesn't romanticise being stateless. It builds the institutions that make the state unnecessary.
quotes.author: Ashanti Alston
quotes.bio: Former Black Panther Party member, former Black Liberation Army soldier, political prisoner 1971–1985. Co-founder of Critical Resistance.
quotes.source: Interview with the Institute for Anarchist Studies

quotes.text: Pan-African social ecology is a way of seeing the world. We do not own the land — the land owns us.
quotes.author: Modibo Kadalie
quotes.bio: Activist scholar, former member of the Detroit-based League of Revolutionary Black Workers. Author of "Pan-African Social Ecology" (2019).

quotes.text: The function of art is to do more than tell it like it is — it's to imagine what is possible.
quotes.author: bell hooks
quotes.bio: Author, critic, and feminist theorist. Wrote forty books, including "Ain't I a Woman?" (1981), "Teaching to Transgress" (1994), and "All About Love" (1999).

quotes.text: Prisons do not disappear social problems. They disappear human beings.
quotes.author: Angela Y. Davis
quotes.bio: Philosopher, activist, abolitionist. Author of "Are Prisons Obsolete?" (2003). Co-founder of Critical Resistance.

quotes.text: We face neither East nor West; we face forward.
quotes.author: Kwame Nkrumah
quotes.bio: First Prime Minister and President of Ghana, leading the country to independence on March 6, 1957. Author of "Neo-Colonialism: The Last Stage of Imperialism" (1965).
quotes.source: Conference of Independent African States, Accra

quotes.text: Language carries culture, and culture carries the entire body of values by which we come to perceive ourselves and our place in the world.
quotes.author: Ngũgĩ wa Thiong'o
quotes.bio: Novelist, playwright, theorist. Wrote in English until 1977, then switched to Gikuyu. Imprisoned without trial by the Moi regime in 1977. "Decolonising the Mind" (1986) is foundational.

quotes.text: The ultimate expression of sovereignty resides, to a large degree, in the power and the capacity to dictate who may live and who must die.
quotes.author: Achille Mbembe
quotes.bio: Historian, political theorist, professor at Wits Institute. Author of "On the Postcolony" (2000) and "Necropolitics" (2019).

quotes.text: I look at an ant and I see myself: a native South African, endowed by nature with a strength much greater than my size so I might cope with the weight of a racism that crushes my spirit.
quotes.author: Miriam Makeba
quotes.bio: Singer, anti-apartheid and civil-rights activist. Stripped of her South African citizenship in 1960. Recorded "Pata Pata" (1967) and over 30 albums. Married Stokely Carmichael in 1968.

quotes.text: Music is a weapon of the future. Music is the weapon of the progressives. Music is the weapon of the givers of life.
quotes.author: Fela Anikulapo-Kuti
quotes.bio: Bandleader, multi-instrumentalist, founder of Afrobeat. His Kalakuta Republic compound was burned down by the Nigerian military in 1977. Recorded over 50 albums including "Zombie" (1976) and "Sorrow Tears and Blood" (1977).

quotes.text: Nobody can give you freedom. Nobody can give you equality or justice or anything. If you're a man, you take it.
quotes.author: Malcolm X
quotes.bio: Born Malcolm Little, also El-Hajj Malik El-Shabazz. Civil rights leader, minister, Pan-African internationalist. Founded the Organization of Afro-American Unity in 1964. Assassinated at the Audubon Ballroom on February 21, 1965.
quotes.source: Speech at the founding of the OAAU

quotes.text: The liberation of women is a fundamental necessity for the revolution, the guarantee of its continuity, and the precondition for its victory.
quotes.author: Samora Machel
quotes.bio: First President of Mozambique, leader of FRELIMO during the war of independence against Portugal. Killed in a still-disputed plane crash on October 19, 1986 inside South African territory.

quotes.text: Many traditional African societies were stateless and acephalous — they organised themselves without rulers, without standing armies, without prisons. The anarchist tradition in Africa is older than the word anarchism.
quotes.author: Sam Mbah
quotes.bio: Lawyer, journalist, and Nigerian anarchist organiser. Co-author with I.E. Igariwey of "African Anarchism: The History of a Movement" (See Sharp Press, 1997).

quotes.text: The decisive features of African society were, generally speaking, communalism and the absence of social classes.
quotes.author: Walter Rodney
quotes.bio: Pan-African historian and political activist. Author of "How Europe Underdeveloped Africa" (1972).

quotes.text: Race doesn't exist. Racism does, and it has a history.
quotes.author: Lilian Thuram
quotes.bio: Footballer turned anti-racism educator. Founded the Lilian Thuram Foundation – Education Against Racism in 2008. Author of "White Thinking" (2019).

quotes.text: You cannot carry out fundamental change without a certain amount of madness. In this case, it comes from nonconformity, the courage to turn your back on the old formulas, the courage to invent the future.
quotes.author: Thomas Sankara
quotes.bio: President of Burkina Faso 1983–1987. Assassinated October 15, 1987.
quotes.source: Quoted in Tafsir Wane, Thomas Sankara: Un espoir assassiné (1989)

quotes.text: Comrades, there is no true social revolution without the liberation of women.
quotes.author: Thomas Sankara
quotes.bio: His Women's Emancipation speech on International Women's Day 1987 remains one of the most radical feminist speeches ever made by a head of state.
quotes.source: Speech on Women's Emancipation, Ouagadougou, March 8 1987

quotes.text: Imperialism is a system of exploitation that occurs not only in the brutal form of those who come with guns to conquer territory. Imperialism often operates in more subtle forms — a loan, food aid, blackmail.
quotes.author: Thomas Sankara
quotes.bio: President of Burkina Faso 1983–1987.
quotes.source: Speech to the UN General Assembly, New York, October 4 1984

quotes.text: We prefer poverty in freedom to wealth in slavery.
quotes.author: Sékou Touré
quotes.bio: First President of Guinea. On September 28, 1958, Guinea was the only French African territory to vote Non to de Gaulle's proposed French Community — choosing immediate independence. France cut all aid overnight, removing even the light bulbs.
quotes.source: Speech rejecting the French Community, Conakry, August 25 1958

quotes.text: In order for nonviolence to work, your opponent must have a conscience.
quotes.author: Kwame Ture (Stokely Carmichael)
quotes.bio: Civil rights leader, co-founder of the Black Power movement. Moved to Guinea in 1969 to work alongside Nkrumah and Sékou Touré. Author of "Black Power" (1967).

quotes.text: Organise, organise, organise. And when you're finished, organise some more.
quotes.author: Kwame Ture (Stokely Carmichael)
quotes.bio: Founding member of the All-African People's Revolutionary Party. Spent the last decades of his life in Guinea organising for pan-African socialism.

quotes.text: Until the lions have their own historians, the history of the hunt will always glorify the hunter.
quotes.author: Chinua Achebe
quotes.bio: Author of "Things Fall Apart" (1958) — the most widely read African novel. Over 20 million copies sold in 57 languages.
quotes.source: Conversations with Chinua Achebe, ed. Bernth Lindfors (1994)

quotes.text: One of the truest tests of integrity is its blunt refusal to be compromised.
quotes.author: Chinua Achebe
quotes.bio: His 1983 essay "The Trouble with Nigeria" remains the sharpest critique of postcolonial failure in Africa.

quotes.text: It is our duty to fight for our freedom. It is our duty to win. We must love each other and support each other. We have nothing to lose but our chains.
quotes.author: Assata Shakur
quotes.bio: Member of the Black Liberation Army. Convicted 1977 in disputed circumstances. Escaped prison 1979, granted asylum in Cuba 1984. Her autobiography (1987) is a foundational text of the Black liberation movement. FBI lists her as a domestic terrorist.

quotes.text: An artist's duty, as far as I'm concerned, is to reflect the times.
quotes.author: Nina Simone
quotes.bio: Pianist, singer, civil rights activist. Wrote "Mississippi Goddam" in one sitting after the murder of Medgar Evers (1963). Went into self-imposed exile, unable to live in the United States.

quotes.text: I'll tell you what freedom is to me: no fear. I mean really, no fear!
quotes.author: Nina Simone
quotes.bio: High Priestess of Soul. Her 1964 Carnegie Hall concert is one of the most important political music performances of the 20th century.
quotes.source: Spoken between songs, Montreux Jazz Festival (1976)

quotes.text: I chose cinema because I could talk to the illiterate as well as the literate.
quotes.author: Ousmane Sembène
quotes.bio: Father of African cinema. Former dockworker and CGT union organiser in Marseille. His film "Black Girl" (1966) was the first sub-Saharan African feature film.

quotes.text: We Africans must stop looking at ourselves through the eyes of others.
quotes.author: Ousmane Sembène
quotes.bio: His novel "God's Bits of Wood" (1960), about the 1947 Dakar–Niger railway workers' strike, is a landmark of African literature.

quotes.text: The man dies in him who keeps silent in the face of tyranny.
quotes.author: Wole Soyinka
quotes.bio: Playwright, novelist, poet. Nobel Prize for Literature 1986. Imprisoned without trial by the Nigerian military for 22 months (1967–69). His prison notes were smuggled out and published as "The Man Died" (1972).
quotes.source: The Man Died: Prison Notes of Wole Soyinka (1972)

quotes.text: No Congolese worthy of the name will ever be able to forget that it was by fighting that independence has been won.
quotes.author: Patrice Lumumba
quotes.bio: First Prime Minister of independent Congo. Delivered this to King Baudouin on June 30 1960 — the most defiant anti-colonial speech ever made in the presence of a sitting colonial monarch.

quotes.text: Cultural identities come from somewhere, have histories. But, like everything which is historical, they undergo constant transformation.
quotes.author: Stuart Hall
quotes.bio: Cultural theorist, founding figure of British cultural studies. Born in Kingston, Jamaica. Founding editor of New Left Review. His work on race, representation, and diaspora is foundational to decolonial media theory.

quotes.text: Every cook can govern.
quotes.author: C.L.R. James
quotes.bio: Historian, political theorist. Author of "The Black Jacobins" (1938) — the definitive history of the Haitian Revolution — and "Beyond a Boundary" (1963). Central figure of Caribbean Pan-Africanism.
quotes.source: "Every Cook Can Govern" — essay in Correspondence (1956)

quotes.text: The rich are only defeated when running for their lives.
quotes.author: C.L.R. James
quotes.bio: His "The Black Jacobins" (1938) on Toussaint L'Ouverture documented the only successful slave revolution in modern history.

quotes.text: Research is formalized curiosity. It is poking and prying with a purpose.
quotes.author: Zora Neale Hurston
quotes.bio: Anthropologist, author, folklorist. Member of the Harlem Renaissance. Author of "Their Eyes Were Watching God" (1937). Died in poverty, buried in an unmarked grave until Alice Walker found it in 1973.

quotes.text: Poetry is a weapon loaded with the future.
quotes.author: Amiri Baraka (LeRoi Jones)
quotes.bio: Poet, playwright, music critic. Founder of the Black Arts Movement. Author of "Blues People" (1963) — a foundational history of Black American music as social testimony.

quotes.text: Your silence will not protect you.
quotes.author: Audre Lorde
quotes.bio: Poet, essayist, activist. Her 1977 MLA lecture became one of the most quoted pieces in Black feminist and queer theory.
quotes.source: The Transformation of Silence into Language and Action (1977)

quotes.text: It is not our differences that divide us. It is our inability to recognize, accept, and celebrate those differences.
quotes.author: Audre Lorde
quotes.bio: Author of "Sister Outsider" (1984), "Zami" (1982), and "The Cancer Journals" (1980).

quotes.text: All that you touch you change. All that you change changes you. The only lasting truth is change.
quotes.author: Octavia E. Butler
quotes.bio: Science fiction novelist. First sci-fi writer to receive a MacArthur Fellowship (1995). Author of "Parable of the Sower" (1993) and "Kindred" (1979). Foundational figure of Afrofuturism.
quotes.source: Parable of the Sower — Earthseed: The Books of the Living (1993)

quotes.text: In order to rise from its own ashes, a phoenix first must burn.
quotes.author: Octavia E. Butler
quotes.bio: Her Parable books are read as political philosophy — anarchists and abolitionists as much as sci-fi readers.

quotes.text: My humanity is bound up in yours, for we can only be human together.
quotes.author: Desmond Tutu
quotes.bio: Archbishop, anti-apartheid activist, Nobel Peace Prize 1984. Chair of the Truth and Reconciliation Commission 1995–2002. Ubuntu — a person is a person through other persons.

quotes.text: There is only one race: the human race.
quotes.author: Robert Sobukwe
quotes.bio: Founder and first president of the Pan Africanist Congress (PAC). Led the anti-pass campaign of March 21, 1960 — the day of the Sharpeville Massacre. Imprisoned under the specially created "Sobukwe Clause", allowing indefinite detention without charge.

quotes.text: I cannot conceive of a greater honour than being in the service of our people.
quotes.author: Chris Hani
quotes.bio: Leader of the South African Communist Party and MK commander. Assassinated April 10, 1993. Mandela's television address that evening is credited with preventing a racial civil war.

quotes.text: The struggle for liberation is a struggle for human dignity.
quotes.author: Eduardo Mondlane
quotes.bio: Founder and first president of FRELIMO. Assassinated by a letter bomb in Dar es Salaam on February 3, 1969.

quotes.text: The first revolution is when you change your mind about how you look at things, and see that there might be another way to look at it that you have not been shown.
quotes.author: Gil Scott-Heron
quotes.bio: Poet and musician. Called "The Godfather of Rap." His spoken word albums "Small Talk at 125th and Lenox" (1970) and "Pieces of a Man" (1971) mapped Black America with devastating clarity.
quotes.source: Interview, Democracy Now! with Amy Goodman (2010)

quotes.text: Music was our weapon against apartheid. Our songs were banned, just like our people.
quotes.author: Hugh Masekela
quotes.bio: Jazz trumpeter, composer. Went into exile in 1960 after Sharpeville and did not return for 30 years. Recorded "Bring Him Back Home (Nelson Mandela)" in 1986.

quotes.text: Before I follow any ideology, it must liberate my people first.
quotes.author: Fela Anikulapo-Kuti
quotes.bio: Bandleader, activist, father of Afrobeat. His mother Funmilayo Ransome-Kuti was thrown from a window by soldiers during the 1977 raid on the Kalakuta Republic.

quotes.text: Emotion is Negro, reason is Hellenic.
quotes.author: Léopold Sédar Senghor
quotes.bio: Poet, philosopher, first President of Senegal (1960–1980). Co-founder of the Négritude movement with Aimé Césaire and Léon Damas. First African member of the Académie française.

quotes.text: The greatest challenge facing all of us is to live as if fear doesn't exist.
quotes.author: Nawal El Saadawi
quotes.bio: Novelist, physician, feminist activist. Author of "Woman at Point Zero" (1975). Imprisoned under Sadat in 1981. Linked patriarchy directly to capitalism and colonialism.

quotes.text: Creativity is the ability to see the relationship between your experience and the collective experience of other human beings.
quotes.author: Nawal El Saadawi
quotes.bio: Author of 50+ books, many banned across the Arab world. Founded the Arab Women's Solidarity Association.

quotes.text: In a fractured age, when cynicism is god, here is a possible heresy: we live by stories, we also live in them.
quotes.author: Ben Okri
quotes.bio: Novelist, poet. Booker Prize winner for "The Famished Road" (1991). His work draws on Yoruba mythology and the relationship between the living and the spirit world.

quotes.text: African unity is no longer a slogan; it is a working reality.
quotes.author: Oliver Tambo
quotes.bio: President of the ANC in exile (1967–1991). Built the global solidarity network that eventually forced South Africa's isolation.
quotes.source: Address to the OAU, Addis Ababa (1983)

quotes.text: I am the product of the masses of my country and the product of my enemy.
quotes.author: Winnie Madikizela-Mandela
quotes.bio: Anti-apartheid activist. Spent years in solitary confinement. Remained uncompromising when others moved toward reconciliation.
quotes.source: Part of My Soul Went with Him (1985)

quotes.text: A civilization that proves incapable of solving the problems it creates is a decadent civilization. A civilization that chooses to close its eyes to its most crucial problems is a stricken civilization. The boomerang effect of colonization works with mathematical precision.
quotes.author: Aimé Césaire
quotes.bio: Poet, co-founder of Négritude. Author of "Discourse on Colonialism" (1950).

quotes.text: The Negro is not. Any more than the white man.
quotes.author: Frantz Fanon
quotes.bio: Psychiatrist, philosopher, revolutionary. Author of "Black Skin, White Masks" (1952).

quotes.text: You have to act as if it were possible to radically transform the world. And you have to do it all the time.
quotes.author: Angela Y. Davis
quotes.bio: Philosopher, activist, abolitionist. Author of "Are Prisons Obsolete?" (2003) and "Women, Race & Class" (1981). Co-founder of Critical Resistance.

quotes.text: The pen is mightiest when it is used to make visible what has been made invisible.
quotes.author: Ngũgĩ wa Thiong'o
quotes.bio: Novelist, playwright. Imprisoned without trial by the Moi regime in 1977. Wrote his first Gikuyu novel on toilet paper while in prison.

quotes.text: Love makes your soul crawl out from its hiding place.
quotes.author: Zora Neale Hurston
quotes.bio: Anthropologist and novelist, pioneer of Black Southern storytelling.

quotes.text: Service to Africa is my guiding principle. Service to Africa is my life.
quotes.author: Robert Sobukwe
quotes.bio: Founder of the PAC. Imprisoned on Robben Island under a clause of Parliament created specifically to hold him indefinitely.
quotes.source: Speech to the PAC inaugural meeting, April 1959

quotes.text: Culture is simultaneously the fruit of a people's history and a determinant of history.
quotes.author: Amílcar Cabral
quotes.bio: Agronomist, revolutionary, theorist of culture as the site of liberation. His "Return to the Source" speech (1970) remains one of the most important essays on culture and decolonization.
quotes.source: Return to the Source: Selected Speeches (1973)

quotes.text: I learned that courage was not the absence of fear, but the triumph over it.
quotes.author: Nelson Mandela
quotes.bio: Anti-apartheid leader. Prisoner on Robben Island 27 years (1964–1990). First democratically elected President of South Africa (1994–1999).
